primary_diagnosis.name: Acute allergic contact dermatitis (nickel/metal-induced) of the infraumbilical abdomen at a belt-buckle contact site, with secondary excoriation and serous weeping
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Allergic contact dermatitis (candidate — parent concept; nickel/metal subtype)

differential.diagnosis: Acute allergic contact dermatitis (nickel/metal-induced), with secondary excoriation
differential.evidence_basis: Intensely pruritic, burning, weeping eruption in a sharply-demarcated rectangular shape corresponding exactly to a new belt's metal buckle, evolving over 4 days from xerosis/scaling — the classic delayed (type IV) metal contact dermatitis pattern, with weeping/crusting from continuous excoriation.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection / impetiginization or cellulitis of the excoriated plaque
differential.evidence_basis: A weeping, crusted, excoriated plaque is a portal for bacterial infection; must be actively screened for (spreading erythema, heat, thick/foul pus, fever, lymphadenopathy) as it cannot be palpated for remotely — the specific complication the telephone triage feared.

Also a pruritic scaly plaque, but typically forms an annular lesion with a raised scaly advancing border and central clearing, and would not conform to a rectangular buckle-shaped geometry — the geometry and intense weeping argue against it.
Can produce oozing and crusting, but is usually not intensely pruritic, tends to show honey-coloured crusts rather than serous weeping, and would not match the geometric buckle-contact distribution.
An atopic flare characteristically involves the flexures and is not sharply confined to the exact footprint of an external object; the lesion here is entirely localised to the buckle-contact site.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a nickel/metal allergic contact dermatitis; the important-not-to-miss consideration is secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated weeping plaque, which drives the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.diagnosis: Acute allergic contact dermatitis (nickel/metal-induced), with secondary excoriation
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile and systemically well, clear serous exudate, redness confined to the geometric patch with no spread, no red streaks or lymphadenopathy — a localised contact dermatitis with excoriation, not an established infective process.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection / impetiginization or cellulitis of the excoriated plaque
differential.evidence_basis: Screened negative now (afebrile, clear exudate, no spreading heat or purulence, no lymphadenopathy) but a foreseeable complication of the ruptured excoriated skin — a mandatory safety-net target, not a current diagnosis.

Remains a lower-probability alternative if the lesion fails to settle on a topical corticosteroid or the geometry is less clear-cut than reported; a persisting/atypical plaque warrants reassessment and possible skin scraping.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes established secondary infection and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable metal contact dermatitis. The prescription-only potent topical corticosteroid and the need to lay eyes on the weeping skin justify prompt review, but NOT urgent same-day ED or 000.

differential.diagnosis: Acute allergic contact dermatitis (nickel/metal-induced) at the belt-buckle contact site, with secondary excoriation and serous weeping
differential.evidence_basis: Classic metal contact dermatitis geometry and time-course; systemically well with a negative secondary-infection screen; the weeping/excoriation and prescription-only potent topical steroid drive allergen avoidance, stopping the irritant antiseptic cream, symptom relief and prompt review rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a common nickel/metal allergic contact dermatitis, patient-performable measures (stop the irritant antiseptic cream, strict metal avoidance, cool compresses, do-not-scratch), a potent topical corticosteroid and an oral antihistamine for the itch, and PROMPT review to obtain the prescription-only steroid — with explicit safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / impetiginization or cellulitis of the excoriated weeping plaque — spreading erythema and heat beyond the geometric patch, thick yellow or foul-smelling pus, red streaks tracking away from the lesion, tender regional lymph nodes, fever or rigors.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The ruptured, weeping, excoriated skin is a portal for bacterial infection; established impetiginization or cellulitis needs topical or oral antibiotics and in-person assessment and cannot be palpated for over telehealth. This is the specific complication the telephone triage feared.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated spreading cellulitis could progress to systemic infection; missed impetiginization prolongs the illness and can spread.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading cellulitis with systemic upset — extensive spreading redness with fever/rigors, feeling generally unwell, or rapidly increasing pain and swelling.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cellulitis with systemic features needs urgent, sometimes intravenous, antibiotics and same-day in-person (potentially emergency-department) assessment — a step up from the localised secondary-infection edge.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading cellulitis with systemic involvement can progress to sepsis.

pathophysiology_summary: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed (type IV, T-cell-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction. In nickel/metal contact dermatitis the allergen is nickel ions released from the metal of the belt buckle in contact with sweaty skin; in a sensitised person, sustained contact drives sensitised T-cells to produce a brisk eczematous response over hours to days, producing a sharply-demarcated pruritic erythematous plaque whose outline mirrors the object — here a rectangular patch on the infraumbilical abdomen exactly where the buckle rests. Starting as xerosis and scaling, continuous scratching (excoriation) breaks the skin barrier and produces serous oozing, crusting and superficial fissuring, and the patient's use of an over-the-counter antiseptic cream (itself a common irritant/sensitiser) has further aggravated the reaction. The process is a localised cutaneous inflammatory one, not systemic or (currently) infective — the patient is afebrile and systemically well with no spreading cellulitic erythema, deep induration, foul purulent discharge or lymphadenopathy. The management priorities are removing the allergen (strict avoidance of the metal buckle), stopping the aggravating antiseptic cream, suppressing the inflammation with a potent topical corticosteroid, symptomatic itch relief, and safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated skin.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the 'rapidly worsening', 'weeping', sleep-preventing rash, the failed OTC creams and the triage note's 'secondary impetiginization / inevitable ED visit' framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the examination and red-flag screen for secondary infection are negative and a common nickel contact dermatitis is telehealth/GP-manageable.

Reading the oozing and light yellowish crust of an excoriated contact dermatitis as bacterial infection and reflexively prescribing antibiotics, when there is no fever, spreading induration, purulence or lymphadenopathy — the exudate is serous, not purulent.

Correctly labelling contact dermatitis but never screening for the one important complication — spreading erythema, purulence, fever, lymphadenopathy — which telehealth cannot palpate for and which separates a benign weeping plaque from an evolving cellulitis.

Failing to identify and stop the OTC antiseptic cream that is aggravating the dermatitis; antiseptic and topical 'itch' creams are common contact irritants/sensitisers and perpetuate an allergic contact dermatitis.

Failing to advise strict avoidance of the metal buckle (or a physical barrier such as tucking the shirt in / a nickel-free buckle), allowing continued exposure that perpetuates the reaction and prevents healing.

Mislabelling a scaly plaque as tinea and prescribing a topical antifungal, missing the geometric external-object correspondence and the intense weeping that point to contact dermatitis; a potent topical steroid is appropriate here precisely because the process is allergic/inflammatory, not fungal.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct examination and palpation of the plaque for warmth, induration or tenderness of an early cellulitis beyond the visible redness
Definitive characterisation of the exudate (serous vs purulent) and of any early secondary infection on the crusted skin
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and respiratory rate without a home device (source in-person values BP 128/82, HR 86, Temp 36.7, RR 16, SpO2 98%, Wt 78 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a nickel/metal allergic contact dermatitis from the geometric buckle-contact history and video; instruct patient-performable measures now (stop the OTC antiseptic cream, strictly avoid the metal buckle or place a fabric barrier, cool damp compresses instead of scratching, an oral antihistamine for the itch); advise a potent topical corticosteroid; and arrange PROMPT review (in person or telehealth prescriber, within ~24-48 hours) to obtain the prescription-only steroid and lay eyes on the weeping skin. Escalation to same-day in-person review or ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers (secondary infection / spreading cellulitis), not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the geometric buckle correspondence and time-course are classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign weeping plaque to ED/000) and UNDER-screening the secondary-infection risk that telehealth cannot palpate for.
